Each method is correct so long as the DM has enough mateยญ rial to smoothly and enjoyably run his adventure session. Allow Time to Design The OM must give himself adeยญ quate time to prepare for an advenยญ ture. Too little time and he cheats his players out of potential enjoyยญ ment. Too much time and not onJy has he created more material that he will use, but he has also cheated himself out of personal time that may have been better spent. [PDF_PAGE 20 | score=10] Create Unusual Puzzles Counterbalance a hack -andยญ slash tendency, by setting up situยญ ations devoid of monsters that require innovative solutions. Dead or Alive? Another option for foiling the combat-crazed is to have some creatures, even evil monsters, be more valuable (or important to gaยญ meplay) alive than dead. Consider the problems caused when a speยญ cific monster is the only source for important information, and the group just killed it. Comic Relief It's only a game, right? Tty tellยญ ing that to the player who is just about to lose his favorite character. Break his tension and get the game back on track by playing up the foiยญ bles of one of the NPCs (or the opยญ ponents). Go for broke
[PDF_PAGE 6 | score=8] Logistics of Play (or another player) should take it upon himself to secure a play area in advance, one that will accom- modate the players and, just as im- portantly, not disturb others who may wish to use the facility. The game session host should ensure that a clean play area and enough seating is available for all anticipated players. If at all possi- ble, arrange for the DM to be seated at a separate table in the gaming area. It's very important that the DM keep his game notes and maps out of the players' sight. Courtesy to the Host At the end of each game session, clean up the play area, regardless of whether the game is played in a student lounge or at a player's home. Toss out food a

But it's worth it-your campaign is ready for the big game today. You've spent over 100 hours devising the penultimate encounter between good and evil (the ultimate battle takes place next week). There '11 be mayhem and carnage galore toยญ night! You've prepared for every possible alternative . . . except one. Your players decide that they really aren't ready for the penultiยญ mate encounter with evil and just want to bash a few more orcs or harass a dragon or two. [PDF_PAGE 16 | score=7] als ahead of time. Know where the players are and what they were doยญ ing last game, be familiar with their foes and up-to-date on the status of the world. As stated previously, advance reยญ view is especially important with purchased or borrowed scenarios. When rulU1ing a game that is not of his own creation, the OM must spend enough time to become thoroughly familiar with its deยญ tails. When a OM creates his own world and adventures, he usually has more background information in mind than he actually writes down. With a purchased or borยญ rowed adventure, this is not the case. [PDF_PAGE 102 | score=6] A common question In the minds of many Dungeon Masters and players is "why do dungeons exist?" In this section) we'll conยญ sider this question and then disยญ cuss some of the most common settings for dungeon based games as well as giving you a few hints on ways to make some very unusual dungeons that your players are sure to reLnember for a long time to come. The Disadvantages of Dungeons There are a number of problems associated with the construction of the vast underground complexes required in a dungeon based campaign. At fu"St glance, each of them seems to be more than adequate justificaยญ tion for the abandonment of the dungeon setting as wholly impracยญ tical. Consider the fo Ilo\\/ing: C
[PDF_PAGE 108 | score=6] Alienation Another aspect of the dungeon style adventure is the fact that it forces the characยญ ters to confront an environment which is quite alien to them. There are numerous hazards which exist underground and not on the surยญ face world. Although any of these hazards might be easily dealt with and discounted in a more familiar s~tting, the fact that they all exist sImultaneously in the vast reaches of the underground can be quite overwhelming to a character. Examples of the ways in which an unfamiliar environment can disorient or confuse a character are unending, but here are a few of the most conunon ones: Keeping Track of Time is very difficult in a dungeon. [PDF_PAGE 46 | score=5] or two to make a conscious deciยญ sion to sacrifice themselves for the sake of their fellows. They get the benefit of heroic death (from which there might be a return) and the adventure can continue from there. One final note--don't let the players realize that the DM has cheated Many players feel manipยญ ulated when they realize that the DM's intervention, not their own skills saved their bacon in a tight situation.
